---
title: "Research"
---

My preference is to organize research around a small number of questions instead of a long list of disconnected topics. Each theme below should stay understandable to a broad audience while still giving specialists enough detail to see where the work is heading.

:::{.card-grid}
::: {.panel}
### Scientific questions

State the core question first. Explain why it matters, what gap it addresses, and what would count as a meaningful result.
:::
::: {.panel}
### Methods and evidence

Summarize the tools, measurements, experiments, models, or imaging approaches you use. This is where visitors understand how you actually work.
:::
::: {.panel}
### Interpretation

Connect the measurements back to the larger scientific story. A good research page is not just a methods list; it explains what the work changes.
:::
:::

## Suggested theme structure

:::{.stack-grid}
::: {.stack-card}
### Theme 1

Write a short paragraph in plain language, then add two or three guiding questions and link outward to papers, datasets, or slides.
:::
::: {.stack-card}
### Theme 2

Use one figure or image when a visual can carry the explanation faster than text. Quarto makes it easy to mix narrative and figures on the same page.
:::
::: {.stack-card}
### Theme 3

Reserve room for work in progress. A site like this is more useful when it can show direction, not just finished outputs.
:::
:::

::: {.note-box}
This page is intentionally lightweight at launch. Replace the theme placeholders above with your actual research areas and add links as papers and project pages come online.
:::
